author: @softreaper217
text:
I'm 34 and i'm disabled (not paraplegic, but not in a shape good enought to use my legs everytime i want to) and live from a disabled allowance in France. I can't work because i can't endure the stress that lead to an arsh degradation of my health that lead to work interuption and, ultimately, being fired (everytime). My old manual wheelchair was a dream (Kuschall Ultralight) but i could have get my hands on it due to a defect from the old one and a really good health insurance at that moment. Things changed, i needed some motorisation later and took the cheapest available (still almost 4k€) and while the electronic and power is good enouth enought (2x200w+ motors with embedded CVT, capped to 6Km/h for legal reasons, 720wh li-po batteries and a control) it's very uncomfortable (you feel every bump and turning angles are totally different from a manual wheelchair) and i barely get out of the house because of this.

All this to say, you gave me an idea, and thank you for this, i dug out my german manual wheelchair from it's grave and i'll take a look at how can i swap all the motorisation from the uncomfortable on to this one (i'll probably need some soldering work, probably with the help of a fablab, but as motorising the old one is 9k€, with less than 3 covered by the insurance, no doubt it'll worth it if i manage to be successfull)

author: @bertjesklotepino
text:
hahaha, China has a bicycle share scheme.
Yeah, so do we in the Netherlands.

But ey, China has an Electric vehicle scheme as well.
Fields full of brand new Electric cars, just rotting away.
But yeah, they do sell a lot of electric vehicles over there. (because all of em in the field were "bought" and so count for having been sold, therefor adding up to the numbers)

Don't believe me, on youtube you have people who show it.
Serpentza for example.

We subsidize them. They put up the windmills and solar panels. We end up with nothing. They will rule.
It is as simple as that, and that is the way the cookie will crumble.
No doubt about it.

text: excellent idea.  here in the states i am appalled by the wasteful government spending of DME, durable medical equipment; old wheelchairs, walkers, crutches, beds, etc... that people get replaced every 3-5 years.   why is there not a government agency that refurbishes that equipment and gives it to another person or send it overseas where it is needed?   most often it ends up in a scrap yard.  when i was a physical therapist i would take old wheel chars from clients who got a new one and use them to make equipment tables for my garage.

author: @scotttovey
text:
There are videos where people couple a hover board to a wheel chair and turn it into an electric wheel chair.

I'm planning on doing the same thing with a wheel chair I pulled out of the dumpster back in 2019 and a hover board I purchased from Walmart on sale for $100. The wheel chair needed a nut and bolt for one of the casters so I purchased an OEM set from the chair manufacturer. It cost $25 including shipping.

Once I have those two things attached and working satisfactory, I'll mount a shopping bag cart on the back of the wheel chair for cargo space.

If I get the mileage 9 mile range the manufacturer claims the board has, I'll be ride it two miles down the road to the hardware store or the dollar store, or even stop by the dairy queen that's a bit further, on occasion.

author: @russellzauner
text:
I worked on a team that made devices that among things would measure heartrate and other metrics - even though several patents were granted through that work, they were never marketed, sold, or even designed as *medical* devices because that triggers several organizations including standards and regulatory along with the not only increased qualification and validation testing requirements but in most cases also chain of custody requirements with components and data used, generated, and or related to the project.  The archival/decommissioning requirements become tracked through a couple agencies and reporting is not optional.
That's why everything was labelled as a "sports performance" or "quantified self" sort of product. 
footnote: at that time a student released their work into an article for the magazine Electrical Design - how to use pulseox type technology to also track changes in blood glucose in a simple sensor package that clips to your earlobe and reads *through* your skin without puncturing it (no needles).  There is a product based on that technology now but the article still exists as well as the components to build the test/prototype device in the article.

author: @Dysiode
text:
I think this is great as a proof of concept, but I imagine there's significantly more from an ergonomics perspective that's necessary and will require input from wheelchair users (the position of the wheels in relation to the arms, the rails on the rim of the wheels to grip, etc). Also, what did you do with the front of the trike? and the back of the trolley? Need to avoid that becoming waste. Finally, it looks like you can get a basic wheelchair in the States for ~$100-300 and at the $300 range they even fold for storage, so I'm not sure it's really a necessary solution for western nations. I'd argue debranding shopping trolleys and using them in multiple places or incentivizing the return of them is a better solution for waste trolleys.

The latter will never happen because giving people money for doing the right thing is seen as a "handout" for the poor (the US did away with returning your quarter when you rent a trolley at an airport because people were gathering them and getting the money back).

author: @malcolm2587
text:
When I was a kid we used to borrow a shopping cart or two from the local alpha beta the wheels have very nice bearings we would take a six or seven inch gutter Spike driving through the bearing into a end of a 2x 4 at both ends
Make two of these take a piece of plywood 3 ft long 18 inches wide fasting 1 perpendicular across pivot one across the front and you have yourself a street luge I grew up in a suburb of Los Angeles where it is very hilly you could reach 35 mph down the hill streets you could lay down headfirst here with your hands or stay with your legs laying back great fun for kids without any money every once in a while you it cost you some skin on your knees and elbows but hey it's fun

Or one of the 2-3kw bldc motors get about 12 20 ah LiFePo4: batteries, parallel and series to get , (12.8 x 6 x40 ah,) and either disconnect all 6 and charge individually, or use , 4 server power supplies, and a cc cv boost converter capable of the voltage and 🤯 boom there's ya charger ! Or use two DC to DC converters and charge 3 at a time, running the  30 volt motors at 60-72v are going to sing! I saw a guy running them at 96v  use a couple 1200-1500 watt controllers. author: @TheIrvy
text:
Absolutely awesome, I'm really happy to hear that so many disabled folk in Thailand are getting access to mobility aids, by hook or by crook.

One important note though, feet plates are very important.  Without them the person using the chair is either going to have to hold their feet up off the floor, which they might not be able to do very well or at all, and if they can't then their poor feet are going to go backwards under the chair and that's just not going to be good for the old hips (or ankles).  You're going to need something that can be folded away so that the person using the chair can get their feet directly on the ground for when they're getting out of the chair, and not have to slide off past them.

author: @Sylfa
text:
Would be better to place the big wheels as far forward as you can without it tipping backwards, then use the small back wheels as tip protectors. That makes it take less energy to move in *and* puts the wheels at a more comfortable placement for pushing.

Of course, an outer grip of some sort would be desirable to avoid getting salt, grit, and road sludge on your hands.

replies.author: @Dysiode
replies.text: Not to mention adjustable foot rests to make getting in and out safer, locking hand brakes the chair user can control, etc. etc.

It's not surprising that most of my shop equipment are on shopping cart wheels.😆
